_meta.authoringNotes: Definitions and management follow Praga Kidney Int 2010 review (PMID 20186145), Perazella Kidney Int 2018 update (PMID 30093082), and KDIGO AKI 2012. Drug-induced AIN epidemiology from Muriithi CJASN 2014 (PMID 24437443) and Schwarz Nephrol Dial Transplant 2000. Checkpoint inhibitor AIN from Cortazar Kidney Int 2016 (PMID 26840066) and Manohar Mayo Clin Proc 2020. PPI association from Lazarus JAMA Intern Med 2016 (PMID 26752246). Steroid response in AIN from Gonzalez Kidney Int 2008 (PMID 18075506). TINU per Mandeville Surv Ophthalmol 2001.

h1Title: Interstitial Nephritis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Interstitial nephritis is inflammation of the kidney's tubulointerstitial compartment with relative sparing of glomeruli and vessels, leading to acute kidney injury (AKI) or progressive chronic kidney disease (CKD). Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) accounts for 15-27% of biopsy-proven AKI in unselected cohorts and up to 70% of drug-induced AKI in some series. Three out of four cases are drug-induced — proton pump inhibitors, NSAIDs, antibiotics (beta-lactams, fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, vancomycin), allopurinol, mesalazine, and immune checkpoint inhibitors lead the list. Infectious AIN (legionella, leptospira, hantavirus, EBV, CMV), autoimmune AIN (lupus, Sjögren, IgG4-related disease, sarcoidosis), and TINU syndrome (tubulointerstitial nephritis with uveitis) make up most of the remainder. Chronic interstitial nephritis follows long-standing exposure to nephrotoxins (analgesics, lithium, heavy metals), genetic disease (cystinosis, sickle cell), and prolonged obstruction. Without prompt withdrawal of the trigger and, often, a course of corticosteroids, AIN progresses to CKD in 30-60% of patients despite adequate baseline therapy. Recognition is critical: many drugs causing AIN are extremely common.

keyStats.prevalence: Acute interstitial nephritis accounts for 15-27% of biopsy-proven acute kidney injury; drug-induced AIN is increasingly common in older adults on polypharmacy
keyStats.demographics: All ages; older adults with polypharmacy at highest risk for drug-induced AIN; pediatric AIN often drug-induced (antibiotics, NSAIDs) or TINU syndrome
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of biopsy-proven AIN approximately 60-70 years (Muriithi 2014); pediatric AIN with TINU syndrome usually in adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: No registry-level global figure; rising incidence parallels global use of PPIs, antibiotics, and immune checkpoint inhibitors

definition: Interstitial nephritis (ICD-10: N12 tubulointerstitial nephritis, acute; N11 chronic interstitial nephritis) is histopathologically defined by interstitial edema (acute) or fibrosis (chronic), an interstitial inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, and macrophages, and varying degrees of tubular damage with sparing of glomeruli and vessels except in autoimmune forms. AIN typically presents 7-10 days after starting a culprit drug (or much later with PPI exposure that can run for months) with rising serum creatinine, sterile pyuria, mild proteinuria (usually under 1 g/day except in NSAID-induced AIN with concurrent minimal change pattern), and sometimes the classic triad of fever, rash, and eosinophilia (now seen in under 10% of cases). Chronic interstitial nephritis develops over months to years from sustained nephrotoxic exposure or systemic disease and presents with insidious creatinine rise, mild proteinuria, anemia disproportionate to GFR, polyuria, sodium wasting, and bland urine sediment. Renal biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis; immunofluorescence is typically negative or shows nonspecific tubular basement membrane staining. Treatment combines immediate withdrawal of any identifiable trigger, supportive care, and corticosteroids in selected cases. Care is led by nephrology, with rheumatology, ophthalmology, oncology, and pharmacy input as appropriate.

typesClassification.description: 70-90% of AIN cases. PPIs, NSAIDs, beta-lactam and other antibiotics, allopurinol, mesalazine, and immune checkpoint inhibitors are the leading culprits. Latency is typically 7-10 days but can extend to months for PPIs and ICIs. Withdrawal of the agent is the cornerstone of treatment.

Includes lupus tubulointerstitial nephritis, Sjögren syndrome AIN, IgG4-related kidney disease, sarcoidosis AIN, and TINU syndrome. Treated with corticosteroids and disease-specific immunosuppression.

Legionella, leptospira, hantavirus, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, brucellosis, EBV, CMV, HIV, BK virus (post-transplant), and adenovirus can cause AIN. Treatment targets the pathogen; rare cases require concurrent steroids.

Predominantly adolescent and young-adult disease; bilateral anterior uveitis develops before, during, or after AIN. Treatment with corticosteroids resolves AIN; ocular disease may recur. Pediatric nephrology and ophthalmology should co-manage.

Long-term exposure to analgesics (analgesic nephropathy), lithium, heavy metals (cadmium, lead), aristolochic acid (Chinese herbal nephropathy, Balkan nephropathy), and chronic obstruction. Often presents with insidious CKD progression; biopsy shows fibrosis and tubular atrophy with chronic inflammation.

primarySymptoms: Insidious rise in serum creatinine and reduction in eGFR over days (acute) to months (chronic), often noted on routine bloods rather than from patient symptoms.
Non-specific systemic symptoms — fatigue, malaise, anorexia, nausea — that frequently accompany AKI of any cause.
Polyuria, nocturia, and increased thirst from impaired urinary concentration capacity, particularly in chronic interstitial nephritis.
Flank pain or loin discomfort from interstitial edema and renal capsule stretching, more often in acute disease.
Fever, maculopapular rash, and arthralgia (the classic AIN triad), present in under 10% of modern drug-induced cases but more frequent with methicillin-class antibiotics and allopurinol.
Bilateral eye redness, photophobia, and blurred vision from anterior uveitis in TINU syndrome — sometimes the presenting feature with subsequent kidney disease.
Anemia disproportionate to GFR in chronic interstitial nephritis, from reduced erythropoietin production by damaged tubules.
Hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis (sometimes hyperkalemic type 4 RTA) from tubular damage and aldosterone resistance.
Hypertension, particularly in chronic disease with established fibrosis.
Symptoms of underlying autoimmune disease (dry eyes, dry mouth, parotid swelling for Sjögren; rash, joint pain for lupus; cough, lymphadenopathy for sarcoidosis).
earlyWarningSigns: Unexpected rise in serum creatinine on routine bloods, particularly 1-3 weeks after starting a new antibiotic, PPI, or NSAID
New sterile pyuria (white cells in urine without bacteria)
Subnephrotic proteinuria with bland urine sediment in a previously healthy adult
Unexplained polyuria and increased thirst in patients on lithium or chronic analgesics
Bilateral red, painful eyes in an adolescent with abnormal urinalysis
emergencySigns: Severe oliguric AKI with hyperkalemia above 6.5 mmol/L, severe acidosis, or fluid overload requiring urgent dialysis
Sepsis with renal compromise in a patient on chronic immunosuppression or with checkpoint inhibitor AIN
Sudden bilateral vision changes with red painful eyes — uveitis requiring urgent ophthalmology assessment
Severe drug hypersensitivity syndrome (DRESS) with AIN — fever, rash, hepatitis, eosinophilia in addition to AKI
Acute pulmonary hemorrhage or hemoptysis suggesting Goodpasture-like overlap or vasculitis as alternative diagnosis

causes.cause: Drug hypersensitivity (proton pump inhibitors, NSAIDs, antibiotics, allopurinol, mesalazine, immune checkpoint inhibitors)
causes.description: Drug-induced AIN is the commonest form. Mechanism is delayed-type hypersensitivity with T-cell activation against tubular antigens cross-reactive with drug haptens. Typical latency 7-10 days for first exposure (or 3-5 days on re-exposure), but PPI and ICI latency can run weeks to months. Recovery often follows drug withdrawal alone.

Anti-PD-1, anti-PD-L1, and anti-CTLA-4 agents (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab, atezolizumab) cause AIN in 2-3% of treated patients, with rising recognition. Pathogenesis involves T-cell activation against renal antigens after immune-checkpoint release. Steroid response is generally favorable; rechallenge after steroid course is possible in selected cases.

Lupus, Sjögren syndrome, IgG4-related kidney disease, ANCA-associated vasculitis, sarcoidosis, and other systemic autoimmune diseases can cause AIN. Treatment combines steroid therapy with disease-specific immunosuppression (rituximab, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide).

causes.cause: Infection (Legionella, leptospira, hantavirus, EBV, CMV, BK virus, TB)
causes.description: Direct invasion or post-infectious immune response causes AIN. Examples include Hantavirus-associated AKI with hemorrhagic fever, BK virus nephropathy post-transplant, and tuberculosis with granulomatous interstitial nephritis. Antimicrobial or antiviral treatment of the underlying infection is the mainstay.

causes.cause: Chronic nephrotoxic exposure (analgesics, lithium, heavy metals, aristolochic acid)
causes.description: Decades of analgesic combinations (phenacetin, paracetamol-aspirin, NSAID combinations) cause analgesic nephropathy with chronic interstitial nephritis and papillary necrosis. Lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis. Aristolochic acid (Chinese herbal nephropathy, Balkan endemic nephropathy) causes rapidly progressive chronic interstitial nephritis and urothelial cancer.
Cystinosis, primary hyperoxaluria, sickle cell disease, and certain congenital tubular disorders cause chronic interstitial nephritis. Treatment depends on the underlying disease.

riskFactors.description: Proton pump inhibitors are the single most common cause of AIN in many recent biopsy series, particularly in older adults on long-term therapy. Risk persists for weeks to months after starting therapy; clinicians should review necessity and dose at intervals.

NSAIDs cause both AIN and acute tubular injury, plus minimal-change-pattern proteinuria in some cases. Risk is higher in older adults, dehydration, and concurrent ACE inhibitor or ARB use ('triple whammy').
Multiple concurrent culprit drugs and reduced baseline GFR raise risk. Pharmacy review at every clinic visit reduces drug-induced AIN in older patients.
2-3% of patients on pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab, or atezolizumab develop AIN, often after several months of treatment. Routine creatinine monitoring during therapy detects early disease.

riskFactors.factor: Active autoimmune disease (lupus, Sjögren, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease)
riskFactors.description: Each can cause AIN as part of systemic involvement. Disease control reduces the risk and severity of renal involvement.
Leptospirosis, hantavirus, schistosomiasis, and tuberculosis in endemic areas can cause AIN with characteristic clinical features.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational and environmental toxin exposure (lead, cadmium, mercury, aristolochic acid)
riskFactors.description: Industrial exposure, contaminated water sources, and certain herbal remedies cause chronic interstitial nephritis. Detailed occupational and dietary history is essential.

affectedDemographics: Older adults with polypharmacy: leading group for biopsy-proven AIN in modern Western series (median age 60-70).
Pediatric AIN: predominantly drug-induced (antibiotics, NSAIDs) or TINU syndrome in adolescents.
Cancer patients on immune checkpoint inhibitors: 2-3% develop AIN, rising in absolute numbers with broader ICI use.
Patients on long-term lithium: chronic interstitial nephritis in 10-20% after 10-20 years of therapy.
Endemic populations (Balkan, parts of China and Southeast Asia): aristolochic-acid-related chronic interstitial nephritis remains a major public health issue.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical suspicion with biochemistry, urinalysis, and renal biopsy. Suspect AIN when an adult has unexplained AKI in the context of a recent culprit drug, sterile pyuria, mild proteinuria (usually under 1 g/day), and bland sediment. Eosinophiluria (Hansel or Wright stain) has poor sensitivity and specificity and is no longer recommended for diagnosis. Renal biopsy is the gold standard and is recommended in adults with unexplained AKI without an obvious cause: it confirms AIN, distinguishes acute from chronic disease, identifies granulomas (sarcoidosis, TB), IgG4-related disease, and helps guide steroid therapy. Light microscopy shows interstitial edema, mixed inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes predominate, with variable eosinophils), tubulitis, and sometimes granulomas. Immunofluorescence is usually negative; positive tubular basement membrane staining suggests anti-TBM antibody disease, IgG4-RKD, or lupus. Electron microscopy shows no immune-complex deposits in typical drug-induced AIN. Laboratory workup includes serum creatinine and eGFR trajectory, full blood count (eosinophilia in some), urine dipstick, urine microscopy, urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio, complement (C3, C4), autoimmune panel (ANA, anti-Ro, anti-La, ANCA), serum IgG subclasses (IgG4 elevated in IgG4-RKD), hepatitis B/C/HIV serology, leptospira and hantavirus serology where epidemiologically relevant, and ACE level (sarcoidosis). Imaging with renal ultrasound rules out obstruction; gallium scan and FDG-PET are occasionally used in suspected granulomatous disease. Ophthalmology evaluation is mandatory in young patients with suspected TINU. A detailed timeline of all prescription and over-the-counter medications, herbal supplements, and recreational drugs is essential — a 'medication forensics' approach.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents AKI severity and onset relative to potential triggers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day. Serial creatinine over days to weeks essential for staging AKI and tracking recovery.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine dipstick, microscopy, and urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies sterile pyuria, white-cell casts, mild proteinuria characteristic of AIN; rules out heavy proteinuria suggestive of glomerular disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spot urine sample analyzed in the local lab. Results within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal biopsy with light, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test; confirms AIN, identifies granulomas, IgG4 disease, and assesses chronic damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anesthesia and ultrasound guidance, 30 minutes plus 6-12 hours observation. Histology report within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Autoimmune panel (ANA, anti-Ro, anti-La, ANCA), IgG4 level, complement, ACE, lysozyme
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies autoimmune causes (lupus, Sjögren, ANCA-associated AIN, IgG4-RKD, sarcoidosis)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 1-2 weeks. Specific findings change management substantially.

diagnosticTests.test: Infection workup (HBV/HCV/HIV serology, leptospira and hantavirus IgM where relevant, BK virus PCR in transplant recipients)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Combination of serology and PCR; results 1-2 weeks at specialist labs.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal ultrasound and selected cross-sectional imaging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out obstruction; assesses kidney size in chronic disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard outpatient imaging; ultrasound 20-30 minutes; CT or MRI in specific cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies anterior uveitis in suspected TINU syndrome and sarcoidosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient ophthalmology referral, 30-45 minutes including dilation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies culprit drug, occupational toxin, herbal supplement, or environmental exposure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Detailed history-taking, pharmacy records, and patient diary; essential to AIN diagnosis.

treatmentOverview: Treatment has three components: withdraw the trigger, support kidney function, and consider corticosteroids in selected cases. Withdrawal of the offending drug (PPI, NSAID, antibiotic, allopurinol, mesalazine, immune checkpoint inhibitor) is the highest-yield intervention. Most patients with mild AIN and rapid drug withdrawal recover renal function over 2-12 weeks without further therapy. Corticosteroids (oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, maximum 60 mg, or IV methylprednisolone 250-500 mg for 3 days then oral taper) are widely used for severe or biopsy-proven AIN that does not improve within 5-7 days of drug withdrawal. Observational evidence (Gonzalez Kidney Int 2008, Muriithi CJASN 2014) supports better recovery and lower CKD rates with steroid therapy started within 1-2 weeks of biopsy, though randomized trials are lacking. Typical course is 4-6 weeks at full dose followed by taper over 8-12 weeks. Immune checkpoint inhibitor nephritis is treated similarly with high-dose corticosteroids; mycophenolate or other immunosuppression added in steroid-refractory cases; rechallenge with the ICI may be possible after steroid response. Autoimmune AIN is treated with disease-specific immunosuppression: rituximab for IgG4-RKD, cyclophosphamide or rituximab for ANCA-associated AIN, mycophenolate or rituximab for lupus AIN, methotrexate or biologic for sarcoidosis. Infectious AIN is treated with the appropriate antimicrobial. Supportive care includes fluid and electrolyte management, dialysis when required for severe AKI, blood pressure control, anemia management with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents in chronic disease, and management of acid-base disturbance. Long-term follow-up includes annual eGFR, urinalysis, and CKD monitoring even after apparent recovery, given the substantial risk of progression to CKD in 30-60% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone of treatment. Discontinue PPI, NSAID, antibiotic, allopurinol, mesalazine, or immune checkpoint inhibitor as soon as AIN is suspected. Most cases improve within 2-12 weeks of withdrawal alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Renal recovery in 50-65% within 8-12 weeks of drug withdrawal alone in mild to moderate cases

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) for 4-6 weeks then taper over 8-12 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: First-line immunosuppression for severe or persistent biopsy-proven AIN. Starting within 1-2 weeks of biopsy is associated with better recovery and lower CKD progression (Gonzalez 2008).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial renal recovery in 70-80% of treated patients; CKD progression reduced compared with withdrawal alone in observational studies

medicalTreatments.name: IV methylprednisolone 250-500 mg/day × 3 days followed by oral taper
medicalTreatments.description: Pulse steroid for severe AIN with dialysis-dependent AKI or rapidly rising creatinine. Followed by oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Faster recovery of renal function in severe cases; risk of infection and hyperglycemia raised

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (1-2 g/day) for refractory or steroid-intolerant AIN
medicalTreatments.description: Used in steroid-refractory AIN, particularly checkpoint-inhibitor nephritis and autoimmune AIN. Allows steroid taper while maintaining immunosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Renal recovery in 50-70% of steroid-refractory cases in observational series

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab for IgG4-related kidney disease and ANCA-associated AIN
medicalTreatments.description: B-cell-depleting therapy used for IgG4-RKD and ANCA-AIN, sometimes for steroid-refractory drug-induced AIN. Doses 375 mg/m2 weekly × 4 or 1 g IV days 1 and 15.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained renal recovery in 70-80% of IgG4-RKD; induction therapy in ANCA disease

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or hemofiltration) for severe AKI
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, fluid overload, or uremia not responding to medical management. Most patients recover sufficient native function to come off dialysis within weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bridge to recovery in 80-90% of AIN patients requiring dialysis at peak; long-term dialysis dependence in 10-20%

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of the underlying infectious or autoimmune cause
medicalTreatments.description: Antibiotic for legionella, antiviral for CMV, antimycobacterials for TB, disease-modifying therapy for lupus or sarcoidosis. Steroids may be added concurrently for severe inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable; tied to underlying disease activity and timeliness of intervention

alternativeTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide (oral or IV pulse) for severe autoimmune AIN
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in vasculitis-associated AIN and severe lupus AIN; reserved for steroid-refractory or rapidly progressive disease. Carries gonadal, infection, and malignancy risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasma exchange in selected severe cases
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in anti-TBM antibody disease and severe vasculitis-associated AIN; evidence is limited for typical drug-induced AIN.

whySeeSpecialist: Any unexplained AKI or progressive CKD with bland urine sediment warrants nephrology referral. Specialists arrange renal biopsy, lead immunosuppression decisions, and coordinate care with oncology (for checkpoint inhibitor AIN), rheumatology (for autoimmune AIN), and infectious diseases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist with access to renal biopsy, electron microscopy, IgG4 staining, and a multidisciplinary team including pathology, rheumatology, and oncology. For checkpoint inhibitor nephritis, prefer a center with integrated onco-nephrology service.
hospitalCriteria: Day-case renal biopsy service with experienced operators
Renal pathology with full panel (light microscopy, immunofluorescence, electron microscopy)
Hemodialysis and continuous renal replacement therapy capability
Multidisciplinary review including pharmacy for drug-cause analysis
keyFacilities: Tertiary nephrology centers with biopsy and dialysis capacity
Onco-nephrology clinics for checkpoint inhibitor disease
Infectious diseases and HIV services for infection-related AIN
Pediatric nephrology and ophthalmology for TINU
preventionStrategies: Review chronic prescriptions regularly and stop or de-prescribe PPIs, NSAIDs, and other AIN-risk drugs when no longer indicated.
Use the lowest effective dose of NSAIDs for the shortest duration possible.
Avoid the 'triple whammy' of NSAID + ACE inhibitor or ARB + diuretic in older patients.
Monitor renal function at baseline and at 2-4 weeks after starting drugs with recognized AIN risk (allopurinol, mesalazine, immune checkpoint inhibitors).
Avoid aristolochic acid-containing herbal remedies and known nephrotoxic occupational exposures.
Maintain hepatitis B vaccination and treat chronic infection (HCV, HIV) to reduce immune-mediated and infectious AIN risk.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain hydration (2-2.5 L water daily) unless restricted by other conditions to support renal recovery.
Track new drugs and supplements, including over-the-counter medications, and discuss with your clinician.
Adhere to corticosteroid taper schedule strictly and never stop suddenly without medical advice.
Avoid further nephrotoxic exposures during recovery (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast where possible, aminoglycosides).
Attend nephrology follow-up appointments for at least 12 months after recovery to detect chronic kidney disease early.
Stop smoking and control hypertension to slow CKD progression.

recommended: Hydration of 2-2.5 L water daily during recovery, less if fluid restriction is required
Moderate protein 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day in CKD stages 3-4 per renal dietitian advice
Adequate calcium and vitamin D during steroid therapy
avoid: Aristolochic acid-containing herbal supplements (some traditional Chinese remedies, Aristolochia clematitis)
Excess high-phosphate processed food in established CKD
Salt over 5 g/day, particularly with hypertension or edema
Recreational drugs and unmonitored supplements during recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate physical activity is safe during recovery. Avoid contact sports during severe AKI or while on intensive immunosuppression. Resistance training during steroid therapy helps preserve lean mass. Patients on chronic dialysis should follow individualized exercise prescriptions from their renal team.
dailyManagement: Take prednisolone with food in the morning; track blood glucose if pre-diabetic or diabetic.
Monitor blood pressure at home twice weekly during recovery.
Drink water steadily through the day; record urine output if instructed.
Avoid any restarted NSAIDs, contrast media, or aminoglycosides during recovery without nephrology approval.
Track weight daily and report sudden change to the clinical team.
Attend scheduled blood tests for creatinine, electrolytes, and full blood count.
prognosis: Outlook depends on cause, severity, and speed of intervention. With prompt drug withdrawal and corticosteroid therapy when indicated, 50-70% of patients with drug-induced AIN achieve complete or near-complete recovery of baseline renal function within 8-12 weeks. However, 30-60% are left with some residual CKD (often stage 2-3), and 10-20% progress to advanced CKD or end-stage renal disease, particularly older adults, those with delayed diagnosis, and those with significant chronic damage on biopsy. Immune checkpoint inhibitor AIN has a favorable steroid response (70-80% recover function); ICI rechallenge after recovery is possible in many patients. Autoimmune AIN outcomes follow the underlying disease — IgG4-RKD responds well to rituximab; ANCA-associated AIN follows vasculitis trajectory. Chronic interstitial nephritis from analgesics, lithium, or heavy metals usually does not reverse but stabilizes with exposure withdrawal and standard CKD care. Predictors of poor outcome are older age, severe AKI at presentation, prolonged drug exposure, significant interstitial fibrosis at biopsy, and pre-existing CKD. Long-term follow-up beyond apparent recovery is essential because residual scarring may not be evident on creatinine alone.
recoveryTimeline: Most drug-induced AIN improves within 2-12 weeks of drug withdrawal. Initial creatinine decline begins within 5-14 days. Corticosteroid response is detectable within 1-2 weeks. Recovery of baseline GFR usually complete within 12 weeks but can continue gradually for 6 months. Long-term CKD risk emerges over months to years; annual nephrology review for at least 2-3 years is recommended even after apparent recovery.
complications: Progression to chronic kidney disease in 30-60% of patients despite recovery from acute episode.
End-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplant in 10-20% of severe or delayed cases.
Recurrence on re-exposure to the original drug or related class; cross-reactivity within beta-lactams or NSAIDs.
Adverse effects of corticosteroids: infection, hyperglycemia, weight gain, mood disturbance, osteoporosis, cataracts.
Permanent visual impairment from uveitis recurrence in TINU syndrome.
Bone disease and anemia in chronic interstitial nephritis from disrupted erythropoietin and vitamin D activation.

supportResources.name: American Society of Nephrology — AKI and AIN resources
supportResources.description: Leading US nephrology society with educational resources for clinicians and patients.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guideline
supportResources.description: International evidence-based guidance covering AIN diagnosis and management within broader AKI care.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Acute Tubular Necrosis and Interstitial Nephritis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing summaries of acute and chronic tubulointerstitial disease.

supportResources.name: International Onco-Nephrology Curriculum
supportResources.description: International Society of Nephrology resources on checkpoint inhibitor nephritis and cancer-related kidney disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Kidney Injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AKI is the clinical syndrome of rapid loss of renal function. AIN is one histological cause of AKI; others include pre-renal, ischemic acute tubular necrosis, glomerulonephritis, and obstruction. AIN requires biopsy and often steroid therapy, while other AKI causes need different specific interventions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CKD is defined by reduced GFR or proteinuria over 3 months from any cause. Chronic interstitial nephritis is one underlying cause; others include diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive nephrosclerosis, and glomerular diseases. Biopsy distinguishes the pattern and guides specific management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pyelonephritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute pyelonephritis is bacterial infection of the kidney with flank pain, fever, chills, and positive urine culture; treated with antibiotics. Interstitial nephritis is sterile inflammation, often drug-induced, with bland urine sediment and no positive culture; treated with drug withdrawal and steroids.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal Tubular Acidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RTA is a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from specific tubular transporter dysfunction. Interstitial nephritis can cause distal or type 4 RTA as one of its manifestations but the diseases are distinct in primary mechanism — RTA is functional, AIN is inflammatory.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Kidney Injury
relatedConditions.relevance: AIN is a leading cause of AKI in modern practice, particularly in older adults on polypharmacy.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Common long-term consequence of unrecognized or recurrent interstitial nephritis.

relatedConditions.name: Renal Tubular Acidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Distal or type 4 RTA can complicate interstitial nephritis and follows the same underlying tubular injury.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Common autoimmune cause of AIN and distal RTA; treatment of the underlying disease improves outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Granulomatous interstitial nephritis is a recognized renal manifestation of sarcoidosis.

faqs.question: What is interstitial nephritis?
faqs.answer: Interstitial nephritis is inflammation of the kidney's tubulointerstitial compartment that spares the glomeruli. Acute interstitial nephritis usually causes acute kidney injury within days to weeks of a triggering drug; chronic interstitial nephritis develops over months to years from sustained nephrotoxin exposure or systemic disease.

faqs.question: What drugs cause interstitial nephritis?
faqs.answer: The leading culprits are proton pump inhibitors, NSAIDs, antibiotics (beta-lactams, sulfonamides, fluoroquinolones, vancomycin), allopurinol, mesalazine, lithium, and immune checkpoint inhibitors. Recognition is essential because all are extremely common in modern practice.

faqs.question: How is interstitial nephritis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines unexplained AKI, sterile pyuria, mild proteinuria, and bland urine sediment with renal biopsy. Biopsy shows interstitial edema, mixed inflammatory infiltrate, and tubulitis with sparing of glomeruli. Eosinophiluria is no longer recommended as a reliable test. Imaging excludes obstruction.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of interstitial nephritis?
faqs.answer: Many patients are asymptomatic and discovered by rising creatinine on routine bloods. Some present with fatigue, malaise, flank pain, polyuria, or — in less than 10% of cases — fever, rash, and eosinophilia (the classic triad). TINU syndrome adds bilateral anterior uveitis.

faqs.question: How is acute interstitial nephritis treated?
faqs.answer: The first step is immediate withdrawal of any culprit drug. Most mild cases recover within 2-12 weeks of withdrawal alone. For severe or persistent disease, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 4-6 weeks with taper over 8-12 weeks improves recovery. IV pulse steroids are used for severe AKI. Underlying autoimmune or infectious causes need specific treatment.

faqs.question: Can interstitial nephritis cause permanent kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even after apparent recovery, 30-60% of patients have residual CKD, and 10-20% progress to advanced CKD or ESRD. Risk factors include older age, delayed diagnosis, severe AKI at presentation, and significant fibrosis on biopsy. Annual nephrology follow-up is recommended for 2-3 years.

faqs.question: Are PPIs really a common cause of interstitial nephritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Proton pump inhibitors are one of the leading drug causes in many modern biopsy series. Risk is highest with long-term use in older adults. Reviewing the necessity of continued PPI therapy and stepping down or switching to alternative acid suppression when appropriate reduces incidence.

faqs.question: What is checkpoint inhibitor nephritis?
faqs.answer: Anti-PD-1, anti-PD-L1, and anti-CTLA-4 cancer immunotherapies cause AIN in 2-3% of treated patients, often months into therapy. Treatment with high-dose corticosteroids leads to recovery in 70-80%; rechallenge with the checkpoint inhibitor is possible in selected cases after steroid response.

faqs.question: What is TINU syndrome?
faqs.answer: TINU (tubulointerstitial nephritis with uveitis) syndrome combines AIN with bilateral anterior uveitis, mainly in adolescents and young adults. Uveitis can precede, accompany, or follow renal disease. Treatment with corticosteroids resolves AIN; ocular disease may recur and needs ophthalmology follow-up.

faqs.question: How is chronic interstitial nephritis different from acute?
faqs.answer: Acute interstitial nephritis develops over days to weeks with edema and mixed inflammation. Chronic interstitial nephritis develops over months to years from sustained nephrotoxin exposure (analgesics, lithium, heavy metals), genetic disease, or chronic infection. It presents with insidious CKD, polyuria, anemia, and shows fibrosis and tubular atrophy on biopsy.

faqs.question: Should steroids be used in every case of AIN?
faqs.answer: No. Mild AIN often recovers with drug withdrawal alone. Steroids are used when AKI is severe, when creatinine does not improve within 5-7 days of withdrawal, or when biopsy shows prominent inflammation. Initiation within 1-2 weeks of biopsy gives best results. Decision is individualized based on severity, comorbidity, and infection risk.

faqs.question: Can interstitial nephritis come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs on re-exposure to the same drug or a closely related agent — for example beta-lactam cross-reactivity. Maintaining a clear allergy list, communicating it to all clinicians, and using alternative therapeutic classes when possible prevents recurrence. Underlying autoimmune AIN can also flare with disease activity.

faqs.question: Is interstitial nephritis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most AIN is acquired (drug-induced, autoimmune, infectious). Some chronic interstitial nephritis is hereditary — cystinosis, primary hyperoxaluria, sickle cell disease, autosomal-dominant tubulointerstitial kidney disease (ADTKD) from MUC1, UMOD, REN, and HNF1B mutations. Genetic testing is offered in families with multiple affected members.

faqs.question: Can autoimmune diseases cause interstitial nephritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lupus, Sjögren syndrome, IgG4-related kidney disease, ANCA-associated vasculitis, and sarcoidosis can each cause AIN. Treatment combines steroids with disease-specific immunosuppression (rituximab for IgG4-RKD, cyclophosphamide or rituximab for ANCA, mycophenolate for lupus).

faqs.question: How long does recovery from AIN take?
faqs.answer: Most patients begin to recover renal function within 1-2 weeks of drug withdrawal and steroid initiation. Creatinine often normalizes within 8-12 weeks but full recovery can extend to 6 months. Some patients have persistent CKD; long-term nephrology follow-up identifies progressive disease early.

faqs.question: Can interstitial nephritis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Risk is reduced by avoiding unnecessary PPIs, NSAIDs, and other AIN-risk drugs, regularly reviewing chronic prescriptions, monitoring creatinine after starting allopurinol or mesalazine, avoiding the 'triple whammy' of NSAID + ACE inhibitor/ARB + diuretic, and steering clear of aristolochic-acid-containing herbal remedies.

faqs.question: Why are NSAIDs particularly risky?
faqs.answer: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow and can directly cause AIN, acute tubular injury, and minimal-change-pattern proteinuria. Risk rises with older age, dehydration, and combined ACE inhibitor or ARB plus diuretic use. Even short courses can cause AKI in vulnerable patients.

faqs.question: Does interstitial nephritis cause hypertension?
faqs.answer: Acute interstitial nephritis usually does not cause hypertension directly. Chronic interstitial nephritis with established fibrosis often does, particularly in analgesic nephropathy and lithium-related disease. Blood pressure control with ACE inhibitor or ARB is standard CKD care after AKI recovery.

faqs.question: Is dialysis ever needed for AIN?
faqs.answer: Yes, in severe AIN with hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, fluid overload, or uremia not responding to medical management. Most patients with AIN-related dialysis-dependent AKI recover sufficient native function to come off dialysis within weeks; 10-20% remain dialysis-dependent.

faqs.question: Can I take PPIs again after recovery?
faqs.answer: Generally PPI is avoided after PPI-induced AIN unless absolutely necessary. Alternative acid suppression (H2 blockers, dietary measures) is used wherever possible. If PPI is later essential, restart at the lowest dose under nephrology guidance with close creatinine monitoring.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after AIN?
faqs.answer: Renal function, blood pressure, and urinalysis are checked every 1-3 months during the first year and at least annually thereafter. Patients with significant residual CKD continue nephrology follow-up indefinitely. Detailed allergy lists prevent re-exposure to the causative drug class.

metaDescription: Interstitial nephritis: drug-induced and autoimmune kidney inflammation causing AKI. Causes, biopsy diagnosis, steroid treatment and prognosis explained.
